note_data.title: Earth and the Solar System
keyPoints: The **Solar System** consists of the Sun, eight planets, moons, asteroids, and comets all held together by gravitational forces
**Earth** is the third planet from the Sun and takes 365.25 days to complete one orbit around the Sun
The **Moon** orbits Earth approximately every 28 days and is responsible for tides on Earth
**Gravity** decreases with distance and is the force that keeps planets in orbit around the Sun
The **phases of the Moon** are caused by the changing positions of the Earth, Moon, and Sun
**Solar eclipses** occur when the Moon passes between the Earth and Sun, casting a shadow on Earth
**Lunar eclipses** occur when Earth passes between the Sun and Moon, casting Earth's shadow on the Moon
**Satellites** can be natural (like the Moon) or artificial (like communication satellites) and orbit due to gravitational force
The **seasons** on Earth are caused by the tilt of Earth's axis as it orbits the Sun

definitions.definition: The Sun and all the objects that orbit around it, including planets, moons, asteroids, and comets
An object that orbits a larger object in space due to gravitational force
When the Moon passes between the Earth and Sun, casting a shadow on part of the Earth's surface
The curved path followed by a planet, moon, or satellite around another object due to gravitational force
The different shapes of the Moon as seen from Earth, caused by the relative positions of Earth, Moon, and Sun

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events or observations without detailed explanation
Give similarities AND differences between two things

modelAnswer.question: Explain why we see different phases of the Moon. [3 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: The Moon orbits around the Earth approximately every 28 days. As the Moon moves in its orbit, different amounts of the Moon's surface are illuminated by the Sun as seen from Earth. This is because the relative positions of the Earth, Moon, and Sun are constantly changing, causing us to see different shapes of the Moon.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Different amounts of Moon's surface illuminated by Sun
Mark 3: Due to changing relative positions of Earth, Moon, and Sun

commonMistakes: Never say 'Earth's shadow causes Moon phases' — phases are caused by changing illumination, not shadows
Students often confuse solar and lunar eclipses — remember solar eclipse means Moon blocks Sun
Don't say 'Moon produces its own light' — Moon reflects light from the Sun
Never write 'planets orbit due to centripetal force' — they orbit due to gravitational force
Students mix up 'waxing' and 'waning' — waxing means getting bigger, waning means getting smaller
examinerTips: This topic appears frequently in Paper 2, especially eclipse and Moon phase questions
Always draw labelled diagrams for eclipse questions to show positions of Sun, Earth, and Moon
Remember to mention 'gravitational force' when explaining orbits
Use the term 'illuminated' not 'lit up' when describing how we see the Moon
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Diagram showing Sun, Earth, and Moon positions for solar eclipse (Moon between Sun and Earth) and lunar eclipse (Earth between Sun and Moon). Also diagram showing Moon phases with Earth at center and Moon positions around it showing new moon, first quarter, full moon, and last quarter.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain why planets closer to the Sun have shorter orbital periods and higher orbital speeds
N/D 2022 P1 Q12 (2 marks) — required identification of the correct order of planets from the Sun and explanation of asteroid belt location
M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — students had to describe the formation of the solar system from a nebula and explain why inner planets are rocky while outer planets are gaseous
N/D 2021 P2 Q5 (4 marks) — tested understanding of Earth's seasons by explaining the tilt of Earth's axis and its effect on daylight hours

workedExample.question: Explain why Mercury has a much shorter year than Earth, but a longer day. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define what 'year' and 'day' mean - year is orbital period around Sun, day is rotational period on axis
Step 2: Explain Mercury's year - Mercury is closer to the Sun, so has smaller orbital radius and higher orbital speed due to stronger gravitational force
Step 3: Apply orbital mechanics - shorter distance and faster speed means Mercury completes one orbit in 88 Earth days
Step 4: Explain Mercury's day - Mercury rotates very slowly on its axis, taking 59 Earth days for one complete rotation
workedExample.answer: Mercury's year is shorter because it orbits closer to the Sun than Earth, experiencing stronger gravitational force which gives it higher orbital speed and a smaller orbital path, completing one orbit in just 88 Earth days. However, Mercury's day is longer because it rotates very slowly on its axis, taking 59 Earth days for one complete rotation, compared to Earth's 24-hour rotation period.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for defining year/day, 1 mark for closer orbit explanation, 1 mark for gravitational force/speed relationship, 1 mark for slow rotation explanation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused rotation and revolution' — always specify whether you're discussing Earth's rotation on its axis (24 hours) or revolution around the Sun (365 days)

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students incorrectly stated that seasons are caused by Earth's varying distance from the Sun' — always explain that seasons result from the 23.5° axial tilt, not orbital distance

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates often mixed up inner and outer planet characteristics' — remember that inner planets (Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars) are rocky and small, while outer planets (Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, Neptune) are gas giants
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the solar system showing: the Sun at center, eight planets in correct order with relative size differences (inner planets small and rocky, outer planets large and gaseous), asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter, and elliptical orbital paths. Include labels for each planet and indicate that outer orbits are more widely spaced. For Earth specifically, show the 23.5° axial tilt and how this creates seasons as Earth orbits the Sun.
note_data.quickRecap: The solar system formed 4.6 billion years ago from a collapsing nebula, with the Sun at the center and eight planets in elliptical orbits. Inner planets are small and rocky, outer planets are large gas giants, separated by the asteroid belt. Earth's 23.5° axial tilt causes seasons, while its 24-hour rotation creates day/night cycles as it orbits the Sun every 365.25 days.
